TRUSTS
FINAL REVIEW OUTLINE

CHAPTER I ▪ INTRODUCTION TO TRUSTS

     Settlor                              Trustee                              Beneficiary
     (grantor)                            (holds legal title)                        (holds equitable title)

                 Transfers property to                Manages property for

                                    Figure 1.A Bifurcated Transfer of Trust Property

There are three parties to a trust, but one person may serve in more than one capacity (e.g., the settlor may
also be a trustee and a beneficiary).
y  Settlor: The creator of the trust (sometimes referred to as the grantor).
y Trustee: The holder of the legal interest in or title to the trust property. The trustee manages, protects,
  and invests certain property (and any income generated therefrom) for the benefit of one or more
  named beneficiaries. A court will appoint a trustee if the settlor fails to designate one or the trustee dies.
y Beneficiary: The holder of the equitable interest, and the person for whose benefit the property is held
   in trust. The beneficiary must expressly or impliedly accept this interest.
To create a trust, the settlor must have intended to do so. A trust is valid as long as it has a trustee, an
ascertainable beneficiary, and assets (e.g., cash, securities, real estate, life insurance policies).
Trust property is divided between principal (i.e., original trust property and any increase in value) and
income (i.e., money generated by trust property).

CHAPTER II ▪ EXPRESS TRUSTS
PRIVATE EXPRESS TRUST
A private express trust clearly states the intention of the settlor to transfer property to a trustee for the
benefit of one or more ascertainable beneficiaries. A private trust is created when a settlor with capacity
to create a trust manifests a present intent to transfer ownership of the property to a trustee who has
duties to perform for the benefit of one or more ascertainable beneficiaries for a valid purpose. No
consideration is required.
Intent
The settlor’s manifestation of intent must occur prior to or simultaneously with the transfer of
property. It can be done orally, in writing, or by conduct—but a writing is required when the Statute
of Frauds applies (e.g., conveyance of real property) or the Statute of Wills applies (e.g., trust created
in a will). Use of common trust terms (e.g., “in trust,” “trustee”) will create a presumption of intent to
create a trust.

pages.normalized_text:
When a donor transfers property to a donee using language that expresses a hope or a wish (rather than
creating a legal obligation) that such property be used for another’s benefit, then it may be considered a
precatory trust (as opposed to an outright gift). But to be a precatory trust:
y The transfer must contain specific instructions to a fiduciary and
y  It must be shown that absent imposition of a trust, there would be an unnatural disposition of the
   donor’s property because of familial relations or a history of support between the donor and the
   intended beneficiary.

          It is important to distinguish between a gift and a trust. A gift is a revocable, outright transfer
      of property without compensation. A trust involves a bifurcated transfer—the settlor gives the
      property to one person for the benefit of another.

Trust Property
Trust property must be (1) identifiable, (2) segregated, and (3) described with reasonable certainty.
The requirement of identifiable trust property (i.e., res) distinguishes a trust from a debt.
A trust also must be funded, meaning that it must contain some property that—at the time the trust
was created—was (1) owned by the settlor and (2) transferred to the trust or the trustee. If a trust that
was invalid for lack of assets is later funded, then a trust arises if the settlor remanifests the intent to
create a trust.

    Know the difference between trusts and debts. A debt can be paid using money from any source,
      while a trust necessarily involves a segregated source of funds.

Valid Trust Purpose
A trust can be created for any purpose that is (1) not illegal, restricted by law, or contrary to public policy,
(2) possible to achieve, and (3) for the benefit of its beneficiaries.
If one of several trust terms violates public policy, then any alternative term will be honored. If no
alternative term is provided, then the problematic term will be stricken. The trust will not fail unless
removal of that term is fatal.
Ascertainable Beneficiaries
Beneficiaries must be identifiable by name (i.e., ascertainable) so that the equitable interest can be
transferred automatically by operation of law and directly benefit the beneficiaries. The settlor may refer to
acts of independent significance to identify the beneficiaries.
However, ascertainable beneficiaries are not required for trusts for the benefit of unborn children, trusts
for the benefit of a reasonably definite class, or charitable trusts.
TYPES OF EXPRESS TRUSTS
Inter Vivos Trusts
Inter vivos trusts are lifetime transfers in trust. A simple declaration of trust will usually suffice to create an
inter vivos trust if the settlor is also the trustee. But if a third-party trustee is named, the declaration must
be accompanied by a delivery in which the settlor parts with dominion and control over the trust property.
No writing is required to transfer personal property to a trust. But a writing is required to transfer
real property, and a court will impose a constructive trust if the writing is lacking. Additionally, evidence
outside of the written agreement (i.e., parol evidence) is admissible to show the settlor’s intent only if the
agreement is ambiguous on its face.

Types of Inter Vivos Trusts
               A will provision that directs the distribution of property to a trust upon the
                    happening of an event.
   Pourover trust     This is permissible—even if the trust instrument was not executed in accordance
                       with the Statute of Wills—if the trust is identified in the will and its terms are set
                          forth in a written instrument.
               A designation given to a bank account in a depositor’s name as trustee for a
                 named beneficiary (no separation of legal and equitable title).
     Totten trust
                      This trust can be revoked by any lifetime act manifesting the depositor’s intent to
                     revoke or by will.
                When proceeds from a life-insurance policy go to the trust upon the insured’s Life-insurance trust                      death. The trust owns the insurance policy, and the trust is irrevocable.
                      This trust arises when a settlor names himself as trustee until death. However, the
      Living trust        settlor can change the successor trustee and beneficiaries until his death.
                       Trust property is not protected from creditors or federal estate taxation.

Testamentary Trusts
A testamentary trust arises when the terms of the trust are written in a will or in a document incorporated
by reference in a will. A testamentary trust must comply with the Statute of Wills. If not, it may still be a
constructive trust or resulting trust, depending on whether it is “secret” or “semi-secret.”
y “Secret” trust: It looks like a testamentary gift but is created in reliance on the named beneficiary’s
  promise to hold and administer the property for another. A constructive trust is imposed on the
   property for the intended beneficiary.
y “Semi-secret” trust: It occurs when a will directs that a gift be held in trust but the testator fails to
  name a beneficiary or specify the terms or purpose of the trust. A resulting trust is imposed on the
   property to be held for the testator’s heirs.
Although most jurisdictions still respect the common-law distinction between “secret” and “semi-secret”
trusts, the modern trend and the Restatement (Third) of Trusts impose a constructive trust in favor of the
intended beneficiaries (if known) in both “secret” and “semi-secret” trust situations.
CHARITABLE TRUSTS
To be a charitable trust, the trust must (1) have a stated charitable purpose and (2) exist for the benefit
of the community at large or for a class of persons in which membership varies. Charitable purposes
include relief of poverty, advancement of education or religion, promotion of good health, governmental
purposes, and other purposes benefiting the community at large.
Charitable trusts are not subject to the Rule Against Perpetuities and may continue indefinitely. And under
the cy pres doctrine, a court may modify a charitable trust to further an alternative charitable purpose if
the original one becomes illegal, impracticable, or impossible to perform.

          If it is difficult to achieve the charitable trust purpose, apply the cy pres doctrine before applying a
      resulting trust.

An honorary trust is a legally enforceable trust that is not created for charitable purposes and has no definite
human beneficiaries. It Is usually created for pets.

CHAPTER III ▪ REMEDIAL TRUSTS
A remedial trust is an equitable remedy created by operation of law; therefore, it is not subject to the
trust-creation requirements, the Statute of Wills, or the Statute of Frauds. The sole duty of the trustee is to
convey the trust property to the beneficiary, so a remedial trust terminates once this duty has been fulfilled.
y Resulting trust: When a trust fails or there is an incomplete disposition of trust property, a court may
   create a resulting trust requiring the holder of the property to return it to the settlor or the settlor’s
   estate to prevent unjust enrichment.
y Constructive trust: This type of trust is imposed when the court concludes that the person holding

A beneficiary’s equitable interest in trust property is freely transferable unless a statute or trust instrument
limits this right. However, once trust property is distributed to the beneficiary, any attempt to restrain the
transferability is generally invalid.
A beneficiary’s creditors can reach trust principal or income only when such amounts become payable
to the beneficiary or are subject to her demand.
SUPPORT TRUST
A support trust directs the trustee to pay income or principal as necessary—including maintaining the
lifestyle to which the beneficiary is accustomed—to support the beneficiary.
Creditors generally cannot reach the assets of a support trust. However, a creditor who provided a
necessity to the beneficiary can be paid directly by the trustee.
MANDATORY VERSUS DISCRETIONARY TRUST
A mandatory trust is one that gives the trustee no discretion regarding payments of income or principal
to the beneficiary. In contrast, a discretionary trust gives the trustee complete discretion regarding
such payments. As a result, the beneficiary of a fully discretionary trust cannot challenge the trustee’s
actions, absent a clear abuse of discretion.
If the trustee exercises his discretion to pay, then the beneficiary’s creditors have the same rights as the
beneficiary. But if the discretion to pay is not exercised, then the beneficiary’s interest cannot be reached
by creditors.

CHAPTER III ▪ REMEDIAL TRUSTS
A remedial trust is an equitable remedy created by operation of law; therefore, it is not subject to the
trust-creation requirements, the Statute of Wills, or the Statute of Frauds. The sole duty of the trustee is to
convey the trust property to the beneficiary, so a remedial trust terminates once this duty has been fulfilled.
y Resulting trust: When a trust fails or there is an incomplete disposition of trust property, a court may
   create a resulting trust requiring the holder of the property to return it to the settlor or the settlor’s
   estate to prevent unjust enrichment.
y Constructive trust: This type of trust is imposed when the court concludes that the person holding
    title to the property, if permitted to keep it, would profit by a wrong or be unjustly enriched. Note that
   wrongful conduct (e.g., fraud, undue influence) is required.

SPENDTHRIFT TRUST
A spendthrift trust expressly restricts the beneficiary’s power to voluntarily or involuntarily transfer her
equitable interest. This restriction applies only while the property remains in the trust. It is inapplicable
once property has been paid out to the beneficiary.
Creditors usually cannot reach a beneficiary’s trust interest if the governing instrument contains a
spendthrift clause. However, the following classes of creditors can still reach a beneficiary’s assets:
y Children and spouses entitled to support
y Holders of federal or state tax liens and
y  In some jurisdictions, providers of necessaries (e.g., health care).

CHAPTER V ▪ TRUST MODIFICATION AND TERMINATION
REVOCABLE TRUSTS
A revocable trust is a trust that can be amended or terminated by the settlor. Under the traditional rule, a
trust is presumed to be irrevocable. But in most jurisdictions and under the Uniform Trust Code (UTC),
a trust is revocable unless the trust documents say otherwise.

          If the essay does not expressly state which rule applies, then discuss the traditional rule and the
      majority (UTC) rule but apply only the majority rule in your analysis.

A settlor can amend or revoke a revocable trust by substantially complying with a method provided within
the terms of the trust. If no such terms are provided, then the trust can be amended or revoked by:
y A later will or codicil that expressly refers to the trust or specifically devises property that otherwise
  would have passed according to the terms of the trust or
y Any other method manifesting clear and convincing evidence of the settlor’s intent.
When a trust is created or funded by multiple settlors, a settlor’s ability to amend or revoke the trust
depends on whether it contains community property.
y Community property: The trust may be revoked by either spouse acting alone, but it can only be
  amended by joint action of both spouses.
y Noncommunity property: Each settlor may revoke or amend the trust with regard to the portion of the
   trust property attributable to that settlor’s contribution.
When a revocable trust is revoked, the trustee must deliver the trust property as the settlor directs. But a
trustee is not liable to the settlor or the settlor’s successors in interest for distributions made, and other
actions taken, under the belief that the trust had not been amended or revoked.
AUTOMATIC TERMINATION
A trust terminates to the extent that:
y The trust is revoked or expires pursuant to its terms
y No purpose of the trust remains to be achieved or
y The purposes of the trust have become unlawful, contrary to public policy, or impossible to achieve.
A trustee may block premature termination if the trust is still serving some material purpose
(e.g., discretionary trusts, support trusts, age-dependent trusts).

Combination and Division of Trusts
A trustee may combine or divide trusts after notice to beneficiaries. However, the result must not impair
the rights of any beneficiary or adversely affect the trust’s purposes.
Revocation by Divorce
Traditionally, a spousal interest created by a trust was not revoked upon divorce. But the current trend
is to treat a spousal interest under a trust similarly to one under a will, so a former spouse is treated as
having disclaimed a trust interest.

CHAPTER VI ▪ PRINCIPAL AND INCOME ALLOCATIONS
Life beneficiaries are generally entitled to trust income, while remainder beneficiaries are generally
entitled to trust principal.
y Stock distribution, whether classified as a dividend or a split, is typically treated as a distribution
   of principal.
y An amount received in exchange for trust property is generally allocated to principal, while an
  amount received for use of trust property is income.
y  Half of the trustee’s compensation, accounting, and other costs is charged to income, while the
   other half is charged to principal.
The trustee must allocate all assets to income or principal and, unless otherwise authorized by the trust
instrument, the allocation must be balanced so that the life and remainder beneficiaries are treated fairly.

                                   Allocation of Trust Property
                        y Any money generated by trust property = Income   Traditional approach                        y Any money generated in connection with a conveyance of trust        (minority rule)                                property = Principal
 Modern/UPAIA approach  y Trustee can recharacterize items and reallocate as reasonably
        (majority rule)          necessary to fulfill the trust purposes

CHAPTER VII ▪ TRUST ADMINISTRATION AND TRUSTEE’S DUTIES
POWERS OF TRUSTEES AND LIABILITY OF THIRD PARTIES
Before any duties are imposed, the trustee must accept the trusteeship. The trustee is then charged with
safeguarding the trust property and otherwise acting as a fiduciary.
The trustee has the powers necessary to act as a reasonably prudent person in managing the trust
(e.g., revoke, withdraw, modify), including the implied power to contract, sell, lease, or transfer the
trust property.
A third party can potentially be held liable for her role in a breach of trust. Therefore, third parties must act
in good faith and give valuable consideration when dealing with trustees. But third parties are not liable if
they act without actual knowledge that such action constitutes a breach of trust.
DUTY OF LOYALTY AND GOOD FAITH
A trustee has the duty to (1) administer the trust in good faith and (2) act reasonably when investing
property and otherwise managing the trust in the best interests of the beneficiaries.

    On the bar exam, determine whether the trustee acted in good faith (subjective standard) and in a
     reasonable manner (objective standard). Good faith alone is not enough.

pages.normalized_text:
Self-Dealing
When a trustee personally engages in a transaction involving trust property, a conflict of interest arises
between the trustee’s duties to the beneficiaries and the trustee’s personal interest.
The following are generally prohibited transactions with trust property:
y Buying or selling trust assets to himself (even at fair market value)
y  Selling property of one trust to another trust that the trustee manages
y Borrowing from or making loans to the trust
y Using trust assets to secure a personal loan
y Engaging in prohibited transactions with friends or relatives
y Otherwise acting for personal gain through the trustee position
When self-dealing is at issue, there is an irrebuttable presumption that the trustee breached the duty
of loyalty. This means that no further inquiry into the trustee’s reasonableness or good faith is needed
because self-dealing is a per se breach.
Even when self-dealing is authorized (by the settlor, court order, or all beneficiaries), the transaction must
still be reasonable and fair to avoid liability for breach.
Conflicts of Interest
If an alleged conflict of interest arises that cannot be characterized as self-dealing, then that conflict is
assessed under the “reasonable and in good faith” standard.
DUTY OF PRUDENCE
Modern law permits a trustee to delegate a duty if it would be unreasonable for the settlor to expect the
trustee to undertake such functions (e.g., investment decisions). If a trustee’s action constitutes a critical
function concerning trust property, then that action is discretionary and not delegable. If the trustee
delegates a duty, then the trustee has a duty to oversee its completion.
Special Skills
A trustee with special skills (e.g., attorney, investment advisor, accountant) is held to a heightened
standard and must use those skills while serving as a trustee.
Trust Investments
The trustee must act as a prudent investor would act when investing his own property, and the trustee
must exercise reasonable care, caution, and skill when investing and managing trust assets. A trustee’s
investments are assessed by looking at the total performance of the trust (i.e., the portfolio approach),
as opposed to individual investments. Factors considered in determining compliance include:
y The trust’s distribution requirements
y General economic conditions
y The investment’s role in relation to the trust’s overall investment portfolio and
y The trust’s need for liquidity, income regularity, and preservation or appreciation of capital.
A trustee’s duties regarding trust investments also include the following:
y Duty to diversify assets: The trustee must adequately diversify trust investments to spread the risk of
   loss unless the administrative costs of doing so would outweigh the benefits.
y Duty to make property productive: The trustee must pursue all possible claims, derive the maximum
  amount of income from investments, sell assets when appropriate, secure insurance, pay expenses,
  and act within a reasonable time in all matters.

y Duty to be impartial: A trustee must be impartial in dealing with beneficiaries of a trust, but this does
   not mean that each beneficiary must be treated equally. Instead, this duty requires that a trustee:
  ○Remain uninfluenced by favoritism or animosity
  ○Balance the interests of present and future beneficiaries by investing property so that it produces a
     reasonable income while preserving the principal for the remaindermen and
  ○Sell trust property within a reasonable time if a failure to diversify would be inconsistent with the
     total-performance portfolio approach.

          If a fact pattern indicates that (1) the trust principal is appreciating but not generating a reasonable
     stream of income or (2) the trust is producing a good amount of income but the principal is
      depreciating, then your analysis should center on the duty of impartiality. In these situations, the
      trustee may be favoring one class of beneficiaries over another.

DUTY TO INFORM AND ACCOUNT
A trustee has a duty to disclose to the beneficiaries complete and accurate information about the nature
and extent of the trust property—including allowing access to trust records and accounts. The trustee
must also provide notice to the beneficiaries if she intends to sell a significant portion of the trust assets.
A trustee also has a duty to periodically account for actions taken on behalf of the trust so that the
trustee’s performance can be assessed against the terms of the trust.
DUTY OF PRUDENT ADMINISTRATION
A trustee also has a duty to administer the trust as a prudent person and must exercise reasonable
care, skill, and caution with regard to trust property. This includes the duty to:
y Secure possession of property within a reasonable time
y Maintain real property and
y Segregate personal property from trust assets (the trustee is only liable for a breach of this duty if it
  causes damage to trust property).
POWERS OF APPOINTMENT
A power of appointment enables the holder (usually the beneficiary) to direct a trustee to distribute
some or all of the trust property without regard to the provisions of the trust.

CHAPTER VIII ▪ TRUSTEE’S LIABILITIES
BENEFICIARIES’ RIGHT OF ENFORCEMENT
The trustee is responsible for lost profits, lost interests, and other losses resulting from a breach of trust.
Beneficiaries may sue the trustee and seek damages or removal of the trustee for such a breach.
LIABILITIES FOR OTHERS’ ACTS
Co-trustees are jointly liable for each other’s acts .
A trustee is not generally liable for a predecessor’s breach, but the successor trustee must take
reasonable steps to compel the predecessor to deliver trust property to the successor and redress any
known breach of trust committed by the predecessor.
The trustee also is generally liable for an agent’s breach if the trustee directed or concealed the agent’s
act or failed to exercise reasonable supervision over the agent.

THIRD PARTIES
A trustee is personally liable on contracts entered into and for tortious acts committed while acting as
the trustee. The trustee can seek indemnity from the trust if the trustee was acting within the scope
of her duties.
When a trustee improperly transfers property as a result of a breach of trust to a third party who is not
a bona fide purchaser, a beneficiary or successor trustee may have the transaction set aside.

CHAPTER IX ▪ RESIGNATION AND REMOVAL OF TRUSTEE
A trustee may resign from the position by (1) providing at least 30 days’ notice to the qualified
beneficiaries, the settlor (if living), and any co-trustees or (2) obtaining court approval.
A trustee may be removed by the court if:
y The trustee becomes incapable of performing his duties
y The trustee materially breaches one or more of his duties
y A conflict of interest arises
y A serious conflict develops between the trustee and any beneficiary (or between co-trustees) or
y The trust is persistently performing poorly because of the trustee’s actions or inaction.

When a future interest in a trust is in the form of a class gift (i.e., a gift to a group of individuals), the
class remains open and may admit new members until:
y At least one class member is entitled to obtain possession of the gift or
y The preceding interest terminates (e.g., the holder of the present life interest dies).
Unless the governing instrument provides otherwise, the general rule is that the gift is expressly limited to
the transferor’s surviving children. This means that the surviving issue of a deceased child do not take.

There are three parties to a trust, but one person may serve in more than one capacity (e.g., the settlor may
also be a trustee and a beneficiary).
y Settlor: The creator of the trust (sometimes referred to as the grantor).

y Trustee: The holder of the legal interest in or title to the trust property. The trustee manages, protects,
and invests certain property (and any income generated therefrom) for the benefit of one or more
named beneficiaries. A court will appoint a trustee if the settlor fails to designate one or the trustee dies.
y Beneficiary: The holder of the equitable interest, and the person for whose benefit the property is held
in trust. The beneficiary must expressly or impliedly accept this interest.
To create a trust, the settlor must have intended to do so. A trust is valid as long as it has a trustee, an
ascertainable beneficiary, and assets (e.g., cash, securities, real estate, life insurance policies).
Trust property is divided between principal (i.e., original trust property and any increase in value) and
income (i.e., money generated by trust property).
A private express trust clearly states the intention of the settlor to transfer property to a trustee for the
benefit of one or more ascertainable beneficiaries. A private trust is created when a settlor with capacity

to create a trust manifests a present intent to transfer ownership of the property to a trustee who has
duties to perform for the benefit of one or more ascertainable beneficiaries for a valid purpose. No
property. It can be done orally, in writing, or by conduct—but a writing is required when the Statute
of Frauds applies (e.g., conveyance of real property) or the Statute of Wills applies (e.g., trust created
in a will). Use of common trust terms (e.g., “in trust,” “trustee”) will create a presumption of intent to
Exam Tips
Definitions
When a donor transfers property to a donee using language that expresses a hope or a wish (rather than
creating a legal obligation) that such property be used for another’s benefit, then it may be considered a
precatory trust (as opposed to an outright gift). But to be a precatory trust:
y The transfer must contain specific instructions to a fiduciary and

y It must be shown that absent imposition of a trust, there would be an unnatural disposition of the
donor’s property because of familial relations or a history of support between the donor and the
It is important to distinguish between a gift and a trust. A gift is a revocable, outright transfer

of property without compensation. A trust involves a bifurcated transfer—the settlor gives the
property to one person for the benefit of another.
Trust property must be (1) identifiable, (2) segregated, and (3) described with reasonable certainty.
The requirement of identifiable trust property (i.e., res) distinguishes a trust from a debt.
A trust also must be funded, meaning that it must contain some property that—at the time the trust
was created—was (1) owned by the settlor and (2) transferred to the trust or the trustee. If a trust that
was invalid for lack of assets is later funded, then a trust arises if the settlor remanifests the intent to
Know the difference between trusts and debts. A debt can be paid using money from any source,
while a trust necessarily involves a segregated source of funds.
A trust can be created for any purpose that is (1) not illegal, restricted by law, or contrary to public policy,
(2) possible to achieve, and (3) for the benefit of its beneficiaries.
If one of several trust terms violates public policy, then any alternative term will be honored. If no
alternative term is provided, then the problematic term will be stricken. The trust will not fail unless
Beneficiaries must be identifiable by name (i.e., ascertainable) so that the equitable interest can be

transferred automatically by operation of law and directly benefit the beneficiaries. The settlor may refer to
acts of independent significance to identify the beneficiaries.
However, ascertainable beneficiaries are not required for trusts for the benefit of unborn children, trusts
for the benefit of a reasonably definite class, or charitable trusts.
Inter vivos trusts are lifetime transfers in trust. A simple declaration of trust will usually suffice to create an
inter vivos trust if the settlor is also the trustee. But if a third-party trustee is named, the declaration must
be accompanied by a delivery in which the settlor parts with dominion and control over the trust property.
No writing is required to transfer personal property to a trust. But a writing is required to transfer
real property, and a court will impose a constructive trust if the writing is lacking. Additionally, evidence
outside of the written agreement (i.e., parol evidence) is admissible to show the settlor’s intent only if the

This is permissible—even if the trust instrument was not executed in accordance
with the Statute of Wills—if the trust is identified in the will and its terms are set
A designation given to a bank account in a depositor’s name as trustee for a
named beneficiary (no separation of legal and equitable title).
This trust can be revoked by any lifetime act manifesting the depositor’s intent to
When proceeds from a life-insurance policy go to the trust upon the insured’s
death. The trust owns the insurance policy, and the trust is irrevocable.
This trust arises when a settlor names himself as trustee until death. However, the
settlor can change the successor trustee and beneficiaries until his death.
Trust property is not protected from creditors or federal estate taxation.
A testamentary trust arises when the terms of the trust are written in a will or in a document incorporated
by reference in a will. A testamentary trust must comply with the Statute of Wills. If not, it may still be a
y “Secret” trust: It looks like a testamentary gift but is created in reliance on the named beneficiary’s

promise to hold and administer the property for another. A constructive trust is imposed on the
property for the intended beneficiary.
y “Semi-secret” trust: It occurs when a will directs that a gift be held in trust but the testator fails to

name a beneficiary or specify the terms or purpose of the trust. A resulting trust is imposed on the
property to be held for the testator’s heirs.
Although most jurisdictions still respect the common-law distinction between “secret” and “semi-secret”
trusts, the modern trend and the Restatement (Third) of Trusts impose a constructive trust in favor of the
intended beneficiaries (if known) in both “secret” and “semi-secret” trust situations.

To be a charitable trust, the trust must (1) have a stated charitable purpose and (2) exist for the benefit
of the community at large or for a class of persons in which membership varies. Charitable purposes
include relief of poverty, advancement of education or religion, promotion of good health, governmental
purposes, and other purposes benefiting the community at large.
Charitable trusts are not subject to the Rule Against Perpetuities and may continue indefinitely. And under

the cy pres doctrine, a court may modify a charitable trust to further an alternative charitable purpose if
the original one becomes illegal, impracticable, or impossible to perform.
If it is difficult to achieve the charitable trust purpose, apply the cy pres doctrine before applying a
An honorary trust is a legally enforceable trust that is not created for charitable purposes and has no definite
human beneficiaries. It Is usually created for pets.
Final Review Outlines | Themis Bar Review | Trusts | 3
A remedial trust is an equitable remedy created by operation of law; therefore, it is not subject to the
trust-creation requirements, the Statute of Wills, or the Statute of Frauds. The sole duty of the trustee is to
convey the trust property to the beneficiary, so a remedial trust terminates once this duty has been fulfilled.
y Resulting trust: When a trust fails or there is an incomplete disposition of trust property, a court may

create a resulting trust requiring the holder of the property to return it to the settlor or the settlor’s
estate to prevent unjust enrichment.
y Constructive trust: This type of trust is imposed when the court concludes that the person holding
title to the property, if permitted to keep it, would profit by a wrong or be unjustly enriched. Note that
wrongful conduct (e.g., fraud, undue influence) is required.
y Gift-over clause: A clause included in some trusts that provides for the disposition of trust property if
the trust purpose fails. Many jurisdictions will honor such a clause before imposing an equitable remedy.
Figure 4.A Alienation of a Beneficiary’s Interest
A beneficiary’s equitable interest in trust property is freely transferable unless a statute or trust instrument

limits this right. However, once trust property is distributed to the beneficiary, any attempt to restrain the
transferability is generally invalid.
A beneficiary’s creditors can reach trust principal or income only when such amounts become payable
to the beneficiary or are subject to her demand.
A support trust directs the trustee to pay income or principal as necessary—including maintaining the
lifestyle to which the beneficiary is accustomed—to support the beneficiary.
Creditors generally cannot reach the assets of a support trust. However, a creditor who provided a
necessity to the beneficiary can be paid directly by the trustee.
A mandatory trust is one that gives the trustee no discretion regarding payments of income or principal
to the beneficiary. In contrast, a discretionary trust gives the trustee complete discretion regarding

such payments. As a result, the beneficiary of a fully discretionary trust cannot challenge the trustee’s
actions, absent a clear abuse of discretion.
If the trustee exercises his discretion to pay, then the beneficiary’s creditors have the same rights as the
beneficiary. But if the discretion to pay is not exercised, then the beneficiary’s interest cannot be reached
4 | Trusts | Themis Bar Review | Final Review Outlines
A spendthrift trust expressly restricts the beneficiary’s power to voluntarily or involuntarily transfer her
equitable interest. This restriction applies only while the property remains in the trust. It is inapplicable
once property has been paid out to the beneficiary.
Creditors usually cannot reach a beneficiary’s trust interest if the governing instrument contains a

spendthrift clause. However, the following classes of creditors can still reach a beneficiary’s assets:
y Children and spouses entitled to support
y Holders of federal or state tax liens and
y In some jurisdictions, providers of necessaries (e.g., health care).
A revocable trust is a trust that can be amended or terminated by the settlor. Under the traditional rule, a
trust is presumed to be irrevocable. But in most jurisdictions and under the Uniform Trust Code (UTC),
a trust is revocable unless the trust documents say otherwise.
If the essay does not expressly state which rule applies, then discuss the traditional rule and the
majority (UTC) rule but apply only the majority rule in your analysis.
A settlor can amend or revoke a revocable trust by substantially complying with a method provided within
the terms of the trust. If no such terms are provided, then the trust can be amended or revoked by:
y A later will or codicil that expressly refers to the trust or specifically devises property that otherwise
would have passed according to the terms of the trust or
y Any other method manifesting clear and convincing evidence of the settlor’s intent.

When a trust is created or funded by multiple settlors, a settlor’s ability to amend or revoke the trust
depends on whether it contains community property.

y Community property: The trust may be revoked by either spouse acting alone, but it can only be
amended by joint action of both spouses.
y Noncommunity property: Each settlor may revoke or amend the trust with regard to the portion of the
trust property attributable to that settlor’s contribution.
When a revocable trust is revoked, the trustee must deliver the trust property as the settlor directs. But a
trustee is not liable to the settlor or the settlor’s successors in interest for distributions made, and other
actions taken, under the belief that the trust had not been amended or revoked.
y The trust is revoked or expires pursuant to its terms
y No purpose of the trust remains to be achieved or
y The purposes of the trust have become unlawful, contrary to public policy, or impossible to achieve.
A trustee may block premature termination if the trust is still serving some material purpose
(e.g., discretionary trusts, support trusts, age-dependent trusts).

If you see a fact pattern involving unforeseen events on the exam, be very clear about what the

Although the beneficiaries’ consent is not required, a beneficiary or trustee may commence a proceeding
seeking court approval or disapproval of such changes.

A trustee may combine or divide trusts after notice to beneficiaries. However, the result must not impair
the rights of any beneficiary or adversely affect the trust’s purposes.
Traditionally, a spousal interest created by a trust was not revoked upon divorce. But the current trend
is to treat a spousal interest under a trust similarly to one under a will, so a former spouse is treated as
Life beneficiaries are generally entitled to trust income, while remainder beneficiaries are generally
y Stock distribution, whether classified as a dividend or a split, is typically treated as a distribution
y An amount received in exchange for trust property is generally allocated to principal, while an
amount received for use of trust property is income.
y Half of the trustee’s compensation, accounting, and other costs is charged to income, while the
The trustee must allocate all assets to income or principal and, unless otherwise authorized by the trust
instrument, the allocation must be balanced so that the life and remainder beneficiaries are treated fairly.

Before any duties are imposed, the trustee must accept the trusteeship. The trustee is then charged with
safeguarding the trust property and otherwise acting as a fiduciary.

The trustee has the powers necessary to act as a reasonably prudent person in managing the trust
(e.g., revoke, withdraw, modify), including the implied power to contract, sell, lease, or transfer the
A third party can potentially be held liable for her role in a breach of trust. Therefore, third parties must act
in good faith and give valuable consideration when dealing with trustees. But third parties are not liable if
they act without actual knowledge that such action constitutes a breach of trust.
A trustee has the duty to (1) administer the trust in good faith and (2) act reasonably when investing
property and otherwise managing the trust in the best interests of the beneficiaries.
On the bar exam, determine whether the trustee acted in good faith (subjective standard) and in a
reasonable manner (objective standard). Good faith alone is not enough.
Final Review Outlines | Themis Bar Review | Trusts | 7
When a trustee personally engages in a transaction involving trust property, a conflict of interest arises
between the trustee’s duties to the beneficiaries and the trustee’s personal interest.
The following are generally prohibited transactions with trust property:
y Buying or selling trust assets to himself (even at fair market value)
y Selling property of one trust to another trust that the trustee manages
y Borrowing from or making loans to the trust

y Using trust assets to secure a personal loan
y Engaging in prohibited transactions with friends or relatives
y Otherwise acting for personal gain through the trustee position
When self-dealing is at issue, there is an irrebuttable presumption that the trustee breached the duty

of loyalty. This means that no further inquiry into the trustee’s reasonableness or good faith is needed
because self-dealing is a per se breach.

Even when self-dealing is authorized (by the settlor, court order, or all beneficiaries), the transaction must
still be reasonable and fair to avoid liability for breach.

If an alleged conflict of interest arises that cannot be characterized as self-dealing, then that conflict is
assessed under the “reasonable and in good faith” standard.
Modern law permits a trustee to delegate a duty if it would be unreasonable for the settlor to expect the
trustee to undertake such functions (e.g., investment decisions). If a trustee’s action constitutes a critical
function concerning trust property, then that action is discretionary and not delegable. If the trustee
delegates a duty, then the trustee has a duty to oversee its completion.
A trustee with special skills (e.g., attorney, investment advisor, accountant) is held to a heightened
standard and must use those skills while serving as a trustee.
The trustee must act as a prudent investor would act when investing his own property, and the trustee
must exercise reasonable care, caution, and skill when investing and managing trust assets. A trustee’s
investments are assessed by looking at the total performance of the trust (i.e., the portfolio approach),
y The investment’s role in relation to the trust’s overall investment portfolio and
y The trust’s need for liquidity, income regularity, and preservation or appreciation of capital.
A trustee’s duties regarding trust investments also include the following:
y Duty to diversify assets: The trustee must adequately diversify trust investments to spread the risk of
loss unless the administrative costs of doing so would outweigh the benefits.
y Duty to make property productive: The trustee must pursue all possible claims, derive the maximum

amount of income from investments, sell assets when appropriate, secure insurance, pay expenses,
and act within a reasonable time in all matters.
8 | Trusts | Themis Bar Review | Final Review Outlines
y Duty to be impartial: A trustee must be impartial in dealing with beneficiaries of a trust, but this does

not mean that each beneficiary must be treated equally. Instead, this duty requires that a trustee:
○Remain uninfluenced by favoritism or animosity
○Balance the interests of present and future beneficiaries by investing property so that it produces a
reasonable income while preserving the principal for the remaindermen and

○Sell trust property within a reasonable time if a failure to diversify would be inconsistent with the
total-performance portfolio approach.
If a fact pattern indicates that (1) the trust principal is appreciating but not generating a reasonable

stream of income or (2) the trust is producing a good amount of income but the principal is
depreciating, then your analysis should center on the duty of impartiality. In these situations, the
trustee may be favoring one class of beneficiaries over another.
A trustee has a duty to disclose to the beneficiaries complete and accurate information about the nature
and extent of the trust property—including allowing access to trust records and accounts. The trustee
must also provide notice to the beneficiaries if she intends to sell a significant portion of the trust assets.
A trustee also has a duty to periodically account for actions taken on behalf of the trust so that the
trustee’s performance can be assessed against the terms of the trust.
A trustee also has a duty to administer the trust as a prudent person and must exercise reasonable

care, skill, and caution with regard to trust property. This includes the duty to:
y Secure possession of property within a reasonable time
y Segregate personal property from trust assets (the trustee is only liable for a breach of this duty if it
A power of appointment enables the holder (usually the beneficiary) to direct a trustee to distribute
some or all of the trust property without regard to the provisions of the trust.
The trustee is responsible for lost profits, lost interests, and other losses resulting from a breach of trust.
Beneficiaries may sue the trustee and seek damages or removal of the trustee for such a breach.
Co-trustees are jointly liable for each other’s acts .
A trustee is not generally liable for a predecessor’s breach, but the successor trustee must take
reasonable steps to compel the predecessor to deliver trust property to the successor and redress any
known breach of trust committed by the predecessor.

The trustee also is generally liable for an agent’s breach if the trustee directed or concealed the agent’s
act or failed to exercise reasonable supervision over the agent.
